title: 10. Portfolio projects
tagline: Turn nine courses of skills into a project a recruiter can poke at.
description: The capstone: choose a route, design an architecture that shows real engineering, and draw it well enough to defend in an interview.

exercises.title: Beyond the thin wrapper
exercises.content:
# Beyond the thin wrapper

Recruiters and hiring managers have now seen a thousand "chat with X" demos. A textbox that forwards prompts to an API — the *thin wrapper* — signals that you followed a tutorial. Here's what signals engineering instead:

### The six signals (you've built all of them)

1. **A real workflow, not a chat.** Your project should *do* something: analyze interview recordings, tailor resumes to job posts, triage support email. Input → processing → artifact.
2. **Retrieval done honestly** (course 04). Private data, real chunking decisions, citations in the output. Bonus: show a case where retrieval fails and how you handle it.
3. **Structured output as contract** (course 03). JSON schemas between components — because something downstream consumes the model's answer.
4. **Visible guardrails** (courses 05, 08). An iteration cap, a tool allowlist, a permission prompt. Nothing says 'production instincts' like an agent that knows when to stop.
5. **Evals in the repo** (course 07). Even 25 cases with scores makes you the candidate who *measures*. Put the eval table in your README.
6. **One production control** (courses 02, 09). A cache with a hit-rate stat, a queue for slow jobs, a cost-per-request note. Show you know tokens are money.

### The three routes

- **🟢 OpenAI RAG app** — private docs, embeddings, retrieval, citations. The workhorse. Differentiate with honest eval numbers and failure handling.
- **🔵 Gemini multimodal app** — screenshots or video in, structured analysis out, with an async queue (course 09) because media is slow. Fewer people build these.
- **🟣 Claude agent workflow** — planner, tools, permissions, memory, trace viewer. Hardest to do well; the trace viewer is what sells it.

### The interview test
Whatever you build, you'll be asked: *why this architecture?* Every box in your diagram needs a one-sentence justification tied to a trade-off you learned here. "I queued video analysis because model calls take minutes and requests time out at 30 seconds" is a hire signal. "The tutorial did it that way" is not.

Next: a quiz to sharpen those justifications, then the whiteboard — draw the architecture you'll actually build.
exercises.attribution: Adapted from the OpenAI Cookbook (MIT), Gemini Cookbook (Apache-2.0), and Claude Quickstarts (MIT)

exercises.title: Knowledge check: defend your design
exercises.content:
# Knowledge check

Three interview-style questions. All three to pass — these are dress rehearsal.

quiz.prompt: An interviewer sees 'chat with your PDF' on two resumes. Candidate A shows response screenshots; candidate B shows an eval table (89% grounded, worst failures listed) and a semantic cache hit-rate. Who reads as the engineer, and why?
options: B — measurement and cost-awareness are what separate building from demoing
quiz.explanation: Same project, different evidence. Evals and ops numbers show you know *how well* it works and *what it costs* — the two questions production teams ask all day.

quiz.prompt: In your architecture, video analysis goes through a job queue. The interviewer asks why. Your best answer:
options: "Analysis takes minutes; HTTP requests time out in seconds — the queue moves slow work out of the request cycle and makes retries safe"
quiz.explanation: The winning answer names the constraint (minutes vs seconds) and the failure it prevents (timeouts, unsafe retries). Every box in your diagram deserves an answer of this shape.

quiz.prompt: You demo an agent project. Which detail most strongly signals production instincts?
options: It shows an iteration cap, a tool allowlist, and a trace viewer for every run
It uses the newest model released this month
quiz.explanation: Guardrails and observability say 'I've thought about how this fails.' Tool count and model version say 'I read the changelog.'

exercises.title: Whiteboard: Draw your capstone architecture
exercises.content:
# Draw a capstone architecture

A strong portfolio project should show a real workflow, not a thin chat wrapper. Use the whiteboard to draw one of these three project routes:

- OpenAI RAG app: private documents, embeddings, retrieval, citations, answer generation.
- Gemini multimodal app: screenshots or videos, analysis queue, model call, reviewed output.
- Claude agent workflow: planner, tools, permissions, memory, trace, and final artifact.

### Your task
Draw the architecture for one route. Include user input, model/API layer, storage, async work, and the final portfolio artifact. Label every arrow — in the interview, each one gets a "why?"

### Source bench
- OpenAI Cookbook
- Gemini Cookbook
- Claude Quickstarts

requirements: Pick one route: OpenAI RAG app, Gemini multimodal app, or Claude agent workflow.
Show the user-facing app, model/API call, and the data or file store.
Add one production control: queue, cache, rate limit, or permission gate.
Label the portfolio artifact a recruiter can inspect.

exercises.hint: A recruiter should be able to see what the user does, where context comes from, which model is called, and what artifact the project produces.
